text: The laparoscopic camera provides a view into the right lower quadrant, where free fluid is visible and suctioned out. Dense adhesions are observed between the appendix, large bowel, small bowel, ovary, and fallopian tubes. The surgeon uses blunt dissection to gently break up these adhesions, taking care to avoid injury to the inflamed bowel. An abscess is entered, and suction is used to contain pus and minimize contamination.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator as a blunt dissecting tool to separate tissues, avoiding grasping inflamed tissue to prevent injury. The appendix is followed down to the cecum, revealing the perforation site. The right colon is mobilized by taking down the white line of TOLT, medializing the colon to better access the appendiceal base and prevent ureteral injuries.

The surgeon uses an energy device to dissect and mobilize the appendix, creating the critical view of safety by tenting up the cecum to evaluate the distance between the appendiceal base and the terminal ileum. Small bites are taken to carefully dissect around the appendiceal base and mesoappendix, using a technique of sweeping up with one hand while pushing down with the other to bluntly enlarge the dissection window. The remainder of the mesoappendix is safely taken with the energy device.

The surgeon achieves a critical view with the appendix elevated, tenting the cecum, and a clear view of the terminal ileum. A stapler with a one millimeter closed staple height is used to secure the appendiceal base, ensuring the staple line is intact and hemostasis is achieved. The right lower quadrant and pelvis are gently irrigated, and omentum is placed over the staple line.
The appendix is placed in an endoscopic retrieval bag and removed from the patient's abdomen, marking the completion of the appendectomy.
